# Windowing ## Learning Objectives After completing this chapter, you should be able to - explain why continuous sensor streams must be segmented into windows, - use reusable windowing code from the `app/` package, - create windows for one activity, - create windows safely per subject and activity, - understand how window size affects live prediction. --- ## Setup ::: {#59f00e3c .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} from pathlib import Path import sys import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt current_path = Path.cwd().resolve() for parent in [current_path] + list(current_path.parents): if (parent / "app").exists(): PROJECT_ROOT = parent break else: raise RuntimeError("Could not find project root containing app/ directory.") sys.path.insert(0, str(PROJECT_ROOT)) from app.config import WINDOW_SIZE from app.data_loader import load_wisdm_phone_accel from app.pipeline import add_magnitude from app.windowing import create_windows, create_grouped_windows DATA_DIR = PROJECT_ROOT / "data" df = load_wisdm_phone_accel(DATA_DIR) df = add_magnitude(df) ``` ::: ::: {.callout-important} ## Software Engineering Principle This chapter does **not** redefine `create_windows()`. Instead, it imports reusable project code from `app.windowing`. This follows the **Don't Repeat Yourself (DRY)** principle. ::: --- ## Why Do We Need Windowing? Wearable sensors produce a continuous stream of measurements. Classical machine learning algorithms expect **fixed-size samples**. Windowing bridges this gap by splitting a continuous stream into equally sized segments. --- ## Step 1 — Create Windows for Walking ::: {#9016b308 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} walking_df = ( df[df["activity"] == "walking"] .sort_values("timestamp") .copy() ) walking_windows = create_windows( walking_df, window_size=WINDOW_SIZE, ) len(walking_windows) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ``` 2798 ``` ::: ::: --- ## Step 2 — Inspect One Window ::: {#157b5aa8 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} first_window = walking_windows[0] first_window.head() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ```{=html}
subject_id activity_code timestamp x y z activity magnitude

303993 1623 A 2520319850830 0.450241 -8.701187 -1.290024 walking 8.807811
303994 1623 A 2520370204834 -0.042175 -9.739563 -1.787415 walking 9.902309
303995 1623 A 2520420558838 3.551483 -10.745132 -1.266403 walking 11.387479
``` ::: ::: ::: {#cbed379a .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} first_window.shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ``` (100, 8) ``` ::: ::: --- ## Step 3 — Visualize One Window ::: {#995bcbab .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4)) plt.plot(first_window["magnitude"]) plt.title("One Walking Window") plt.xlabel("Sample") plt.ylabel("Magnitude") plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](04_windowing_files/figure-html/cell-6-output-1.png){width=809 height=377} ::: ::: --- ## Step 4 — Create Windows for the Entire Dataset ::: {#d60ed1ab .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} all_windows = create_grouped_windows( df, window_size=WINDOW_SIZE, ) len(all_windows) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ``` 8066 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.callout-warning} ## Common Pitfall Always create windows separately for each subject and activity. Otherwise, a window may contain multiple activities. ::: --- ## Checkpoint 1. Why do we need windowing? 2. What does a 100-sample window represent at 20 Hz? 3. Why should windows not span multiple activities? 4. Why is the window size relevant for live prediction? --- ## Summary In this chapter you learned - why windowing is necessary, - how reusable project code is imported, - how to create windows safely, - why window size controls the trade-off between stability and responsiveness. In the next chapter we will extract numerical features from every window.
